title: Induction Talk for New Food Bank Volunteers

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Hello everyone, and thank you so much for coming along to volunteer at the Meadowfield Community Food Bank. My name's Sandra, I'm the volunteer coordinator, and I'll spend the next few minutes explaining how we work and what your week might look like.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: First, a little background. We started eight years ago in the back room of a church, and we now support around three hundred families every week. We're entirely run by volunteers — there's just one paid member of staff, and that's our warehouse manager. Everyone else, including me, gives their time for free.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Let me walk you through our weekly routine, because different tasks happen on different days. Deliveries from the supermarkets arrive first thing on Monday, so Monday is our busiest day for unloading and stacking. If you enjoy being active and don't mind lifting, Monday's the day for you.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Sorting the food — that's checking dates, dividing items into categories and sending anything out of date for recycling — we do that on Tuesday. It's quieter and a good job if you'd rather sit at a table and chat while you work.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Packing the parcels themselves happens on Thursday. We make up boxes tailored to each family — some need baby food, some need pet food, and so on. And then the parcels are handed out to families on Friday, which is when we open our doors to the public, so Friday is the day we most need friendly, welcoming people on the front desk.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Now, a really important point about what we can and can't accept. We're always short of tinned vegetables, pasta and rice, and long-life milk — those are gold dust to us. What we cannot take, and this surprises people, is anything homemade or home-cooked, for food-safety reasons. Fresh fruit and vegetables we can take, but only in very small amounts, as they don't keep. So the single most useful thing you can donate is tinned and dried food with a long shelf life.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: A few practical matters. There's tea and coffee in the kitchen, free of course, and we ask everyone to sign the register when they arrive and when they leave — that's a health-and-safety requirement, not us checking up on you. Please wear closed-toe shoes, as there's a lot of lifting, and we'll provide an apron and gloves.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: If you can't make your shift, all we ask is that you let us know the day before if you possibly can, so we can find cover. You don't need to explain why. And if you'd like to take on more responsibility later, do speak to me — we're always looking for people to train as shift leaders, and there's a short course for that.

turns.speaker: Sandra
turns.text: Finally, the thing I most want you to remember: treat everyone who comes through our doors with warmth and dignity. Many of the families are here for the first time and feel embarrassed. A kind word matters just as much as the food. Right — let me show you around the warehouse.
